summary:
- **Core topic/purpose:** Petition (and subsequent memorial) by **Weng Cheong & Co. and many other goldsmiths/jewellers/bullion merchants** seeking **repeal or amendment** of **Sections 36 and 36A** of the **Straits Settlements Minor Offences Ordinance (Chapter 24)**, especially the rule restricting melting/altering precious-metal articles for **three days** after receipt.  
- **Geography/time period:** **Straits Settlements** (notably **Singapore, Penang, Malacca**); main actions and correspondence span **1938–May 1940** (supplemental memorial dated **31 Jan 1940**; ordinance assented **6 Mar 1940**; Colonial Office draft reply **11 May 1940**).  
- **Key legal change/event:** A bill amending the Minor Offences Ordinance was **unanimously passed by the Legislative Council on 12 Feb 1940** and became **Ordinance No. 7 of 1940** (effective date noted as **11 Mar 1940**). It **repealed and re-enacted** Sections **36 and 36A** and added **36B**.  
- **Main complaints/proposals by petitioners:**  
  - Object to **Section 36** requiring a **3-day holding period** before melting/altering/defacing/putting away received goods/metals/articles (unless police permission), arguing it **immobilises capital** and exposes them to **market fluctuation risk**; they cite an English comparison of **48 hours** (Old Metal Dealers’ Act 1861).  
  - Request either **removal** of the “within three days of receipt” wording or reduction to **1 day** or **no more than 2 days**.  
- **Registration issue resolved in part:** Earlier objections included **compulsory registration** of workers/dealers in platinum/gold/silver and alleged **unequal enforcement** (banks vs. goldsmiths/bullion merchants). The revised provisions reportedly **exclude bullion merchants** from registration and **exclude employees** (via **Section 36B**); petitioners state they accept **registration in principle** if it is “ministerial.”  
- **Government/Colonial Office positions and decisions:**  
  - The **Governor (Sir Shenton Thomas)** argues local complainants are often **~24 hours late** reporting thefts; police circularisation takes time, so **48 hours is the minimum** and **3 days is “barely sufficient,”** and he **does not recommend amendment**.  
  - A **Colonial Office draft despatch** (Downing Street, **11 May 1940**) to the Governor acknowledges doubts about “handicapping” a traditional trade; suggests a **trial reduction to 2 days** if the Governor agrees, but otherwise states there is **insufficient cause to intervene**.  
- **Key actors/organizations:** Petitioners led by **Weng Cheong & Co.** and numerous named firms; **Straits Settlements Government**, **Legislative Council**, **Chief Police Officer** (permission authority under s.36), **Governor & Commander-in-Chief Sir Shenton Thomas**, and the **Colonial Office/Secretary of State for the Colonies (Malcolm MacDonald, M.P.)**.  
- **Notable numbers/penalties:** Section 36 penalty up to **$500 fine** and/or **6 months’ imprisonment**; Section 36A penalty up to **$200 fine** and/or **3 months’ imprisonment**; disputed holding period **3 days** vs requested **1–2 days** (and English reference **48 hours**).
